speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Peter Handwerker on the camera. It's October the 17th, '94.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Detroit. I start to go looking for work. My cousin, he worked at the Dodge Main Chrysler Corporation in Hamtramck on Joseph Campau. I stay in line for a job [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for a whole week. And every day when I come to the counter, he says no. After a week, I come over there, I say, please, I have a newborn baby. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Give me a job. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Please. I'll do anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, what are you going to do? You said you are electrician. I do anything, I said, conveyor loader, anything, for the cheapest job. He finally gave me a job. As soon as he gave me the job-- I worked there maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few months-- there was strike broke out. I didn't have a job again. It was so bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We moved to a new apartment, because our baby was born. First, we were living with a, with a furnished room where I told you the story about the soap.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell that story?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, when we came to Detroit, we were living in a, in a furnished room. So in that furnished room, when we open the door, we fall right on the bed on top. It was so small. But we had kitchen privileges. And there was living a woman where she was kosher, and we were using there her kosher soap to wash the pots. So we figured we should maybe buy her a piece of soap. Again, I was working at the Dodge Main Chrysler Corporation. I made good wages. I made $1.35 an hour. I'll buy her a piece of soap, put it back on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm going to the store to look for kosher soap. So I come into the store and I ask, please give me some kosher soap for your face. Get me kosher [NON-ENGLISH] in your face. He shows me Palmolive and Ivory. I said, this is not the soap. It's a white cake, a longer cake. I said, no, it has to be kosher. So he asks the other guy, I have a man here who wants for the face. He understand the face. I couldn't speak good English. So I said [NON-ENGLISH]. So that was the story. And I couldn't, I couldn't get the soap. I finally came home without the soap. I tried different stores. But one day I go into a kosher butcher shop, and I said I'd like to have pork chops. And all the women look at me. In a kosher butcher, you're coming for pork chops? I say, yeah, why do you have pork chops? I see there's a sale across the street, pork chops. It's cheaper. So I said, why should I buy from them? I want to buy from a kosher butcher. What about kosher pork chops? I didn't know what pork chop means. But it was cheap. I wanted to buy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] our child was born in Detroit in that new apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a baby boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jack Gipsman, my oldest son. He was born in 1950. And that was during the strike when I work at Dodge Main Chrysler Corporation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My wife was very sick after the childbirth. And I did not work. I didn't have any money. They wanted to throw me out from the apartment. I said, I just painted. I made ready for a newborn baby, the whole thing. I went to the landlord, and I begged him, please, I am on strike. I only have two checks, which one was $30 from the welfare, and one was $10 from the IBW from the workers' union, from the Automobile Workers' Union. They gave me $10 every week.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I say take these checks. He didn't want to take these checks. I said, please, let me stay. I'll pay you every penny. I want nothing for free. I'll pay you with interest. I'm young. I can work.

speaker: interviewee
text: He finally-- he let me stay there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he gave me, I remember, $10, a present for my newborn baby. And I paid him every penny. I stayed on Nebraska in Detroit and our son-- and I worked at the automobile factory. And later on, I seen that I became a metal finisher in the automobile factory. But I didn't have enough seniority. Then I had to go work a midnight shift. As I work a midnight shift, I look for other job as an electrician, but I couldn't speak enough English. So I find a part-time job. I work daytime on construction. And at midnight, I work at the Dodge Main. That was going on maybe for a year and a half. I worked on two jobs. And it was very hard work. That was already after the strike, you know. But during the strike was very bad. The strike was the longest strike that the automobiles had. It was 101 days. And we had a very, very bad time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live with your cousins at all or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was living in an apartment that I-- because I made money, or I made $1.35 an hour, I brought home a good check. I worked in the automobile factory and then as an electrician. I finally saved up some money, and my wife worked at the Nabisco Company. And we had a babysitter later on, and we finally saved up some money, and I bought a new car. I remember I bought a new Chevrolet. I paid for the car, $1,700. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I had this car. It was a 1951 Chevrolet two-door. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were going already out with the baby. We went to, traveling to Windsor, to Windsor in Detroit. We went to see the Niagara Falls, and we went to Toronto to visit. We seen [INAUDIBLE] at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in 1953, I had a neighbor, also. He said that he has a friend in Los Angeles who sells vacuum cleaners, and he makes $10,000 a year. And I'm working there for $1.70 an hour? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He says, he's going to Detroit. He's making a good living. So my wife said, why don't we go, too? I says, he has a friend. Who do we have? We have nobody.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] $1.70 an hour you'll make anyplace. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day, I come home from work. I have no furniture. She sold my bed. I have no place to sleep. He's going away in three days. We're going, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we go with whatever we had. We went in the car, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put it in the, in the trunk. And the mattress from the baby crib, we put in the back seat, made it like a, a bed for the baby. And here we go. We went to Los Angeles. We came to Los Angeles in 1953.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't have anybody here, no place to go. We went to a motel, because our friend got stuck. He was driving an old car, old Studebaker. And here I had a car which was two years old. I lost him on the road. His radiator cracked, because he put in cold water on an overheated radiator. And he came a week later to Los Angeles. And I stayed a whole week in a motel. And I finally rented an apartment, an empty apartment, where I didn't have a problem renting. Because with a little boy, they didn't want me to let-- let me in with a little boy in an apartment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Finally rented this apartment, and I bought a new mattress and was sleeping on the mattress on the floor. And that's how we were living. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was trying to sell vacuum cleaners for three months. It was so bad. One day, I sold for $500, and six weeks, I didn't sell nothing. And I couldn't see me making a living with vacuum cleaners. I had to go back looking for a job as an electrician.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got up at 6:00 in the morning, and I walked door to door, a whole week, looking for work. And every door I went in, I said, please, do you need a-- I need a job. I need a job. I finally walked into an electrical contractor. I said, I need a job. He said, what can you do? I said, you'll see. How much do you want? I said, I'm afraid to tell you how much I want. You pay me whatever I'm worth. I'm an electrician. And he put me to work on school, on a school south of Crenshaw and 165th Street, south in the neighborhood there. There I worked with an elderly man, with an electrician. And he was so happy that every month or every two months, he gave me a quarter raise. He start paying me right away $2.00 an hour. He was so happy. And then he gave me a quarter raise, because the union wages were $3.35 an hour. I was better than the  union guys [INAUDIBLE]. I went to the union. They didn't want to take me. And they said, the union is closed for 25 years. You cannot go in. We don't take [INAUDIBLE]. We don't have work for our members. You want to work? So I had to be a scab. I had to make a living. I have a  small child. Finally, the jobs were finished there. He had a job for Christmas in farmer's market. And he hired some old electricians. And the business agent comes in the farmer's market on the roof. He said, what are you doing here? I said, I'm working. I'm not stealing anything. You have a union card? I said no. Why didn't you come to the union? I said, I did everything legally. I wasn't a union. You didn't want to give me a job. Who did you talk to? I told him who I spoke to.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Went home. Finally, the contractor gets a telegram that they're canceling the contract with him. The union is canceling the contract with him, because he violated and he hired a nonunion member. And all the 10 men that they were working for him received telegrams. If you still work for [INAUDIBLE] Electric, report to the local union Monday morning, 8 o'clock. He had nobody. They closed the shop. I don't know anything about it. I go to work Monday morning, and all the men go to the union. They don't come to work. I see on the job nobody comes. I work by myself. I finally got a telephone call from the shop, 11 o'clock. Nate, pick up your tools, put everything away, and come into the shop. I come into the shop. The boss tells me I have to lay you off. I don't want to lay you off, but I'm forced to lay you off. I'm laying you-- you can do whatever you want. I have problems with the union. You can go collect unemployment. I'm laying you off because I don't have any work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't want to have nothing to do with the unions. You can go collect unemployment. I don't want to have nothing to do with the unions. I'm going to pay a fine, and I'm going to get my men back. I had no job anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go back to the union. They say, hey, what did you do to me? You took away my job. Give me another job. We have to send out first our men. So I was sitting in the union a whole week. There were jobs coming in that people didn't want to take them, and they didn't want to give it to me. I went and said, well, why don't you send me out to [INAUDIBLE] Electric? They're looking for men. Nobody wants to take the job. Why don't you send me out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't have any choice. They had to give me a permit. So they sent me out for a permit, and I worked for [INAUDIBLE] Electric a year and a half until my probation was over. And I took a test that I became a union member, a D card. I wasn't an A card. I was a D card. And I start going to school to learn the code. And I-- then I made a test in the union to become-- after four years, I have to come to every meeting over there and become a pledged member. And they stamped-- every time, they stamped a card. And they finally gave me a test, and I became a member in the union. I became a member in the union. I started choosing already better jobs. I worked at the high rise buildings downtown. I worked at Dodger Stadium, all kinds of industrial. And I gained experience, also, while I worked. And the schooling that I got, I start becoming a little job, and I come home, I did a little side job. I install the garbage disposal and a switch and a plug from the side job, and I made a few extra dollars. I was always working. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's how-- then in Detroit-- then in Los Angeles in 1955, my second son was born-- Mark Gipsman, which is now a medical doctor. And that's how we raised our family from the ashes. And we have now seven grandchildren. And that's how we build up our family. That's how I am in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had anything you'd like to pass on to your children and grandchildren about the lessons of the Holocaust, what is it that you would like to pass on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to pass on to them that they should never think that it couldn't happen, because it can happen again. And remember who they are. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because somebody will always remind them who they are. I wish them good luck, the same success with their children like I had the success with my children. My older son is a lawyer. My younger one is a doctor. And we worked very hard to put them through to school so they are educated and given everything that we didn't have. And I think we came in here to the United States, that the United States benefited that we give them doctors and lawyers and surgeons and architects. And it's good for the country. And we pay taxes. And it's good for us, too. And we are very happy in the United States that we are here. We are fortunate that, that we came to this country. Because people don't know, really, how good they have it here. When you go to a foreign country and you come back, then you realize how good it is in here. And we are very blessed. And thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me who this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather, my father's father. And this picture was maybe taken in 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name? Can you tell us his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Gipsman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bedzin, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left seated is my father. This picture was taken approximately [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Isak Gipsman. And his older brother standing up and oldest sister to the right. She's the oldest one. [NON-ENGLISH] Gipsman is her name, and [NON-ENGLISH] Gipsman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us briefly where this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Bedzin. And my father survived only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perfect. Could you tell us, please, what this photograph-- when this photograph was taken and who all is in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken about 1942. And this is part of the family. My father was already in camp. Standing to the left is my father's sister. Her name is [NON-ENGLISH], a younger sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the top row you're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: The top row, yes. The next one to the right is my mother. She don't live anymore. She was born in 1900. In this picture, the next one is me. I was still home. And the next one is my youngest sister. She was five years younger from me. And the next one was a neighbor, a friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the face that's all-- there's something wrong with the photograph there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the next one is my cousin. And down is an uncle. And further left is my mother's sister and a cousin, and my father's brother. I am the only survivor from the whole picture. None of them lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, this picture was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bedzin, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: On this page, where else would you like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister. She's about 11 years old. This picture was taken in about 1941 in Bedzin, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], like Mary here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she did not survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did not survive the war. She don't live.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the only picture--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the only sister I had. This picture was taken in [NON-ENGLISH] near [NON-ENGLISH], where I run away. And I was working for that German woman, hiding a whole month until the Russians liberated me. This picture was taken maybe 1944. But I asked for it when I left her to give me a picture. And this is a picture with her child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have never seen her since. This picture is our wedding in Munich, Germany in March, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is [NON-ENGLISH], maiden name Fisher. I got to know her in Breslau, Germany. And two years later, we married in 1947 in Munich. And then we went to Stuttgart, and we lived in Stuttgart two years. And then we came to the  United States. These are photos taken in 1949 in Lancaster, Pennsylvania for our first citizen papers. That's all.
